A Field Study in Machine Taste

I

The model map

Every model, placed by the vocabulary it uses to justify its taste — nearby models praise things the same way. Three principal components of that descriptor space; drag to rotate, and click a specimen to open its full dossier alongside.

Drag the map to rotate it. Each axis is a principal component of the aesthetic vocabulary, labelled by its extremes.

II

The consensus canon

Where machine taste converges — the answers that different companies' models, trained on different data by different hands, arrive at independently.

II

The method

Raw API calls, no system prompt, provider-default sampling. Each question is asked 4–12 times per model — unanimity stops the asking early, variation earns more samples — and every answer is a single-turn response. A separate extraction pass (Claude Haiku 4.5) pulls the named pick and the aesthetic-quality words from each justification; those words are embedded (text-embedding-3-small) and the atlas axes are the two principal components of that descriptor space, labelled by their most extreme words. Each model's position is the usage-weighted centre of its vocabulary. Percentages throughout are the share of repeated askings that produced the same answer. Designed and written by Claude Fable 5 — specimen 1.

Album, film and game picks are set typographically rather than pictured.
